author: AI didn't replace coding, it replaced typing
text:
Normal people with AI:

"Write my email."
"Summarize this PDF."
"Create a LinkedIn post."

✨ Done in 30 seconds.
Developers with AI at 3 AM:
"Why did you change 47 files?"
"Who told you to delete the database?"
"That's not what I meant by optimize."
"Please stop helping."

AI didn't replace coding.
It replaced typing.

Now we spend our nights:
🌱 Planting prompts
🐛 Harvesting bugs
☕ Irrigating with coffee
🙏 Praying before deployment

The future they promised:
"AI will do the work."

The reality:
"Now I manage three interns that never sleep."

Follow Rahul Choudhary for more

author: Tharun J
text: 🔓 Passwords are the keys to your digital kingdom. The question isn't whether your passwords are strong—it's whether they're strong enough before attackers test them. Secure proactively, not reactively. author: Kuber Mehta
text: "If you have nothing to hide, you have nothing to fear" This has been the most common justification of the government banning Telegram in India during the NEET examination to avoid leaks. But the point most people miss is that privacy isn’t about being paranoid or locking your data with hardware keys, it’s about principle. Private platforms matter because a scary amount of information about you is constantly collected, profiled, and sold. We already see the consequences every day through hyper-targeted advertising, spam calls, and algorithmic profiling. For me and for a lot of founders and startups working in AI, Telegram offered the most streamlined agent setup and operation This ban has legitimately broken my claw adjacent workflows I use for my AI work, which literally delayed the project I was intending to release next week and an insane amount of actual work by other companies. And yes, this did put anyone seriously building chat-interface agents in India at a real disadvantage. Call it whatever you want, but private, secure, non-disclosure communication platforms aren’t protecting just criminals they’re protecting ordinary people.
